JS From Zero EP.12 — Loops: for, while, and do...while
Day twelve of being reborn into JavaScript, and this time we're talking about loops.
A loop is basically there to take a highly repetitive action and repeat it over and over until some condition is met. Here's the 0 to 10 example loop I wrote in an earlier chapter:

With this example you can't really feel why loops matter. But what if you needed to output 0 to 1000? Typing out 0 to 1000 by hand would be exhausting, and you'd probably make a typo somewhere along the way. That's exactly where loops shine — we can knock out all those repetitive actions in one shot. Put in the right condition and you get 0 to 1000 in no time. It also cuts down on
JavaScript has three basic loops to start with ↓
The slightly more formal name for a loop is a
(There are other loops too, which I'll cover later — here I'm just doing the basics.)
loop is the most basic and most common one. You usually write it with three parameters, so let me use the example above to show it ↓
The idea is simple. Each time the condition check passes, it runs the code inside the curly braces, then updates the current starting value. In plain terms: in the image above the starting value is

, which we know isn't true, so the loop breaks and we jump out of it.
That's the basic flow of how the loop runs (sorry for the rough drawing) ↓

Day twelve of being reborn into JavaScript, and this time we're talking about loops. We'll go through the three basic ones — for, while, and do...while — how they actually run, and the classic beginner trap of writing an infinite loop by forgetting to update the counter.
